items.title: Internal hemorrhoids as the most common cause of HDB
items.contentHtml:
Internal hemorrhoids

  • Internal hemorrhoids are the most common cause of lower gastrointestinal bleeding (LGIB), responsible for ~40% of cases.
  • The bleeding is bright red, painless, typically with straining, and is usually self-limiting.
  • The diagnosis is confirmed with proctosigmoidoscopy or anoscopy; complete colonoscopy is reserved to rule out other lesions.
  • The first-line treatment for persistent bleeding is elastic band ligation (success rate >80%).
  • Trap: in patients >50 years, do not attribute the bleeding only to hemorrhoids without ruling out colorectal neoplasia.

items.foco: Angiodysplasia in the right colon and cecum
items.title: Angiodysplasia: vascular lesion in the right colon
items.contentHtml:
Angiodysplasia of the right colon

  • Angiodysplasia is an acquired vascular ectasia, most frequently located in the right colon and cecum (up to 80% of cases).
  • It is associated with chronic kidney disease, aortic stenosis (Heyde syndrome) and advanced age (>60 years).
  • Bleeding is usually recurrent, painless and low volume; may present as massive hemorrhage on rare occasions.
  • Diagnosis of choice: colonoscopy showing cherry red, flat, 2-10 mm lesions with star vessels.
  • Endoscopic treatment: argon plasma coagulation (APC) or hemostatic clip.
  • Trap: angiodysplasia can be difficult to identify if the colon is not adequately clean; repeat colonoscopy if there is suspicion.

items.title: Meckel's diverticulum: cause of HDB in children
items.contentHtml:
Meckel's diverticulum in pediatrics

  • The Meckel's diverticulum is the most common congenital anomaly of the gastrointestinal tract (rule of 2: 2% population, 2 inches, 2 feet of the ileocecal valve, 2 types of ectopic mucosa –gastric and pancreatic–).
  • It is the most common cause of massive HDB in children <2 years; bleeding occurs due to peptic ulcer in the adjacent ectopic gastric mucosa.
  • The pain is usually painless or mild cramping; the bleeding is bright red or currant jelly-like (if massive).
  • Diagnosis: scintigraphy with Tc-99m pertechnetate (sensitivity ~85-90% in children; lower in adults).
  • Surgical treatment: resection of the diverticulum (diverticulectomy) with primary anastomosis.

items.title: Diverticulosis: painless massive bleeding
items.contentHtml:
Diverticulosis and hemorrhage

  • Diverticulosis is the second most common cause of HDB in adults >60 years; The bleeding is painless and massive (hematochezia).
  • It occurs due to rupture of the vasa recta arteriole in the neck of the diverticulum, with loss of >1000 mL in 24 hours in 50% of cases.
  • Most frequent location of bleeding: left colon (sigmoid), but massive bleeding is usually from the right colon.
  • Diagnosis: Urgent colonoscopy (ideally within 24 hours) to identify the bleeding diverticulum and treat with hemostatic clip or argon plasma coagulation.
  • If endoscopy fails, angiography with selective embolization or surgery.
  • Trap: do not confuse with diverticulitis (pain + fever); There is no pain here.

items.title: Colonoscopy as a diagnostic gold standard
items.contentHtml:
Colonoscopy in HDB

  • Colonoscopy is the diagnostic method of choice in acute and chronic HDB, with sensitivity >90% to detect the source of bleeding.
  • It allows direct visualization of the mucosa, identification of lesions (diverticula, angiodysplasia, neoplasms, hemorrhoids) and simultaneous hemostatic therapy.
  • Ideally it should be performed after intestinal preparation with polyethylene glycol (4-6 L) for proper cleaning; in active bleeding it can be done with faster preparation (1 L in 1 h).
  • The diagnostic success rate is higher if it is performed within the first 24 h of admission (sensitivity >80% vs. 50% after 48 h).
  • Trap: in unstable massive bleeding, colonoscopy is contraindicated; prioritize angiography or surgery.

items.foco: Mesenteric ischemia in elderly people with pain
items.title: Mesenteric ischemia: abdominal pain and HDB
items.contentHtml:
Acute mesenteric ischemia

  • Mesenteric ischemia (due to arterial or venous occlusion) occurs in elderly people with cardiovascular risk factors, and presents with intense abdominal pain disproportionate to the examination.
  • Rectal bleeding is usually late and scant (hematochezia or occult blood) due to mucosal necrosis; pain precedes bleeding.
  • Diagnosis: abdominal CT angiography with contrast (sensitivity >90%) shows arterial or venous filling defect, thickening of loops with pneumatosis.
  • Serum marker: elevated lactate >2 mmol/L (nonspecific) and metabolic acidosis.
  • Treatment: early revascularization (embolectomy, bypass, thrombolysis) and resection of necrotic intestine if necessary.
  • Trap: do not wait for the bleeding to be massive to suspect it; pain is the key.

items.title: Colorectal cancer: occult and chronic bleeding
items.contentHtml:
Colorectal cancer as a cause of HDB

  • Colorectal cancer is a common cause of chronic and occult HDB, manifesting as occult blood in feces or iron deficiency anemia.
  • Bleeding is intermittent, low volume, and is usually associated with changes in bowel habits (constipation/diarrhea) and weight loss.
  • The most frequent location it is sigmoid colon (25-30% of cases), followed by rectum.
  • Diagnosis: colonoscopy with biopsy; if not possible, CT colonography (sensitivity >85% for lesions >10 mm).
  • Trap: in patients >50 years of age with unexplained iron deficiency anemia, always rule out colorectal cancer; Do not attribute to hemorrhoids.

items.foco: Scintigraphy with marked red blood cells for localization
items.title: Scintigraphy with labeled red blood cells in HDB
items.contentHtml:
Scintigraphy with labeled red blood cells

  • scintigraphy with labeled red blood cells (technetium-99m) is a nuclear medicine technique to locate active bleeding at a rate >0.1 mL/min.
  • It is useful when colonoscopy is not possible or does not identify the source, and allows bleeding to be monitored for 24 hours (images serial).
  • Sensitivity ~85-90% for active bleeding, but low specificity to identify the exact lesion (only localizes the intestinal segment).
  • Main indication: intermittent massive bleeding or bleeding of uncertain location after negative colonoscopy.
  • Trap: a negative result does not rule out bleeding if it has stopped; The scan is only positive if there is active bleeding during the acquisition.

items.title: Diagnostic and therapeutic angiography
items.contentHtml:
Angiography in HDB

  • Angiography is indicated in active bleeding with a rate >0.5 mL/min, allowing the source to be located and selective embolization to be performed.
  • Sensitivity >90% if the bleeding is active at the time of the study; the specificity is high (95%).
  • It is performed by selective catheterization of the superior and inferior mesenteric artery; the key finding is contrast extravasation in the intestinal lumen.
  • Embolization with microparticles or coils has technical success >80%, with risk of intestinal ischemia (<5%).
  • Snare: in unstable patients, angiography can delay surgery; consider stabilization first and then angiography if resources are available.

items.title: Proctosigmoidoscopy in anorectal pathology
items.contentHtml:
Proctosigmoidoscopy

  • Proctosigmoidoscopy is the procedure of choice to evaluate internal hemorrhoids, anal fissures, fistulas and low rectal neoplasms.
  • It allows visualization of up to 25-30 cm from the anal verge, without the need for complete bowel preparation (only a cleansing enema).
  • Indicated in patients with bright red bleeding, anal pain or mass. palpable; It is complementary to colonoscopy if it is negative.
  • Key finding: internal hemorrhoids (grades I-IV) or anal fissure (linear ulcer in the posterior midline).
  • Trap: do not perform in the presence of intense pain (suspect acute fissure or abscess); best examination under anesthesia.

items.foco: Infectious cause: colitis due to E. coli O157:H7
items.title: Colitis due to E. coli O157:H7
items.contentHtml:
Hemorrhagic colitis due to E. coli O157:H7

  • Colitis due to E. coli O157:H7 is an infectious cause of HDB, characterized by bloody diarrhea (hematochezia) and crampy abdominal pain, without fever or with low-grade fever.
  • It is associated with the consumption of undercooked meat or unpasteurized milk; the incubation period is 3-5 days.
  • Serious complication: hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS) in children and the elderly (triad: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, acute renal failure).
  • Diagnosis: stool culture with specific medium (MacConkey agar with sorbitol); The colonies do not ferment sorbitol.
  • Treatment: supportive measures and avoid antibiotics (they increase the risk of HUS).

items.foco: Sigmoid volvulus in the elderly with distension
items.title: Sigmoid volvulus: distension and obstruction
items.contentHtml:
Sigmoid volvulus

  • Sigmoid volvulus is a torsion of the sigmoid colon on its mesenteric axis, which causes intestinal obstruction and can lead to ischemia and bleeding.
  • It typically occurs in elderly people with chronic constipation or neurological disease, with severe abdominal distention, pain and absence of bowel movements/gas.
  • Rectal bleeding is usually late. and scant (due to mucosal necrosis), but it can be massive if there is perforation.
  • Diagnosis: abdominal x-ray (coffee bean sign) and abdominal CT (mesenteric whirlwind sign).
  • Initial treatment: endoscopic decompression (rectosigmoidoscopy with rectal probe placement) with success rate >80%; if it fails or there are signs of ischemia, surgery (sigmoid resection).
